extra.message:
DOM-based Cross-Site Scripting via URL hash fragment

The HTML_PREFIX contains inline JavaScript that reads document.location.hash, decodes it with decodeURIComponent, and writes it directly into the DOM using document.write without sanitization, enabling DOM-based XSS.

Impact: An attacker can craft a URL with a malicious hash fragment to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser.

Fix: Use textContent or innerText instead of document.write, and sanitize the hash fragment before inserting into the DOM.

Missing Authentication on All Endpoints

The do_GET handler processes all requests without any authentication check. There is no session validation — the SESSIONID cookie is set on login but never verified on subsequent requests. All functionality (database queries, file access, command execution, comments) is accessible to unauthenticated users.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can access all application functionality including executing commands, reading files, querying the database, and performing administrative actions.

Fix: Implement session validation by checking the SESSIONID cookie against a server-side session store before processing sensitive requests.

SQL Injection via id parameter (Blind Boolean/Time-based and UNION)

The 'id' parameter is directly concatenated into a SQL query without any sanitization or parameterization. An attacker can perform blind boolean-based, time-based, and UNION-based SQL injection to extract all data from the database, including user credentials (PII).

Impact: Full database compromise including extraction of all usernames and passwords (PII). Attacker can read arbitrary data from the SQLite database.

Fix: Use parameterized queries: cursor.execute('SELECT id, username, name, surname FROM users WHERE id=?', (params['id'],))

Arbitrary Code Execution via pickle deserialization

User-supplied data from the 'object' parameter is deserialized using pickle.loads without any validation. Pickle deserialization of untrusted data allows arbitrary code execution.

Impact: Full remote code execution. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload that executes arbitrary Python code (e.g., os.system) on the server.

Fix: Never deserialize untrusted data with pickle. Use safe serialization formats like JSON. If pickle is required, implement a restricted unpickler.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via 'path' parameter

When the 'path' parameter contains '://', the application uses urllib.request.urlopen to fetch the URL, allowing an attacker to make the server request arbitrary internal or external resources.

Impact: An attacker can scan internal network services, access cloud metadata endpoints, or interact with internal APIs that are not publicly accessible.

Fix: Implement URL allowlisting, block private/internal IP ranges, and restrict allowed protocols.

Arbitrary Code Execution via subprocess (Command Injection)

The 'domain' parameter is directly concatenated into a shell command executed via subprocess.run with shell=True, allowing command injection via shell metacharacters (;, |, &&, backticks, $()).

Impact: Full remote code execution. An attacker can execute arbitrary OS commands on the server with the privileges of the application process.

Fix: Use subprocess.run(['nslookup', params['domain']], shell=False) to avoid shell interpretation, and validate the domain parameter.

XML External Entity (XXE) Injection - Local and Remote File Read

User-supplied XML is parsed with lxml using load_dtd=True, resolve_entities=True, and no_network=False. This allows XXE attacks to read local files (file:// protocol) and make outbound HTTP requests (remote XXE).

Impact: An attacker can read arbitrary local files (e.g., /etc/passwd, application source code) and perform SSRF via remote entity resolution.

Fix: Disable DTD loading and entity resolution: use XMLParser(resolve_entities=False, no_network=True, load_dtd=False) or defusedxml library.

XPath Injection via 'name' parameter

The 'name' parameter is directly interpolated into an XPath expression using string formatting, allowing XPath injection to extract data from the XML document including passwords.

Impact: An attacker can manipulate the XPath query to extract sensitive data such as passwords from the USERS_XML document using blind boolean-based XPath injection.

Fix: Use parameterized XPath queries or validate/sanitize the name parameter to prevent injection of XPath metacharacters.

Path Traversal via 'include' parameter (Local File Inclusion)

The 'include' parameter is used to open local files without path validation when it doesn't contain '://'. An attacker can traverse directories to read (and execute) arbitrary local files.

Impact: An attacker can read arbitrary local files and have their content executed as Python code, leading to full system compromise.

Fix: Validate the include path against a whitelist of allowed files. Ensure the resolved path is within an allowed directory.

HTTP Header Injection (CRLF Injection) via 'charset' parameter

The 'charset' parameter is directly inserted into the Content-Type HTTP response header without sanitization. An attacker can inject CRLF characters (%0D%0A) to add arbitrary HTTP headers or split the response.

Impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary HTTP headers, set malicious cookies, disable security headers (X-XSS-Protection), or perform response splitting to inject arbitrary content.

Fix: Validate the charset parameter against a whitelist of allowed character encodings. Strip or reject any input containing CR/LF characters.

Cleartext Storage of Passwords in Database

User passwords are stored in plaintext in the SQLite database without any hashing. The passwords from USERS_XML are inserted directly into the users table.

Impact: If the database is compromised (e.g., via SQL injection), all user passwords are immediately exposed in plaintext without any need for cracking.

Fix: Hash passwords using a strong, salted hashing algorithm (bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2) before storing them in the database.

Denial of Service via Memory Exhaustion in 'size' parameter

The 'size' parameter is used to allocate a string of size*size characters without any upper bound validation, allowing an attacker to exhaust server memory.

Impact: An attacker can send a request with a very large size value (e.g., size=9999999) to consume all available memory, crashing the server and denying service to legitimate users.

Fix: Implement an upper bound check on the size parameter (e.g., max 1000) and reject requests exceeding the limit.

Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Operations

The comment submission endpoint (and all other state-changing operations) accepts GET requests with no CSRF token validation. An attacker can forge requests that create comments on behalf of victims.

Impact: An attacker can trick authenticated users into performing unintended actions (posting comments, etc.) by embedding malicious requests in images or links on external sites.

Fix: Implement CSRF tokens for all state-changing operations. Use POST method for state-changing requests and validate a unique token per session.

Open Redirect via 'redir' parameter

The 'redir' parameter value is directly inserted into a meta refresh tag without any URL validation, allowing an attacker to redirect users to arbitrary external sites.

Impact: An attacker can craft URLs that redirect users to phishing sites or malicious pages, leveraging the trust of the legitimate domain.

Fix: Validate redirect URLs against a whitelist of allowed destinations. Only allow relative redirects or same-domain URLs.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint

The /login endpoint has no rate limiting or account lockout mechanism, allowing unlimited authentication attempts for brute-force attacks.

Impact: An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force or dictionary attacks against user accounts to discover valid credentials.

Fix: Implement rate limiting (e.g., max 5 attempts per minute per IP/username), account lockout after repeated failures, and CAPTCHA after several failed attempts.

Weak Session Management - SESSIONID without HttpOnly or Secure flags

The SESSIONID cookie is set via a meta http-equiv tag without HttpOnly or Secure flags. The session ID is also generated using random.sample() which is not cryptographically secure.

Impact: The session cookie can be stolen via XSS attacks (no HttpOnly flag) and intercepted over unencrypted connections (no Secure flag). The weak PRNG makes session IDs potentially predictable.

Fix: Set HttpOnly and Secure flags on the session cookie. Use secrets.token_urlsafe() for cryptographically secure session ID generation. Set cookies via Set-Cookie HTTP header instead of meta tags.

Information Disclosure via Exception Traceback

When an exception occurs, the full Python traceback is returned to the user, potentially exposing internal file paths, code structure, library versions, and other sensitive information.

Impact: Attackers can learn about the application's internal structure, file paths, library versions, and code logic to craft more targeted attacks.

Fix: Return generic error messages to users. Log detailed tracebacks server-side only.
